data.title: Red-Hot Singapore Index Has Room to Run on Global Uncertainties — Analysis
data.description:
By Amanda Lee

Singapore's benchmark equities index is fresh off a record high, helped by a resurgence in the city's heavyweight bank stocks in a time of higher-for-longer global interest rates. New global uncertainties and local measures to boost trading liquidity could add to the gains, analysts said.

The FTSE Straits Times Index notched its highest-ever close Wednesday. Even after a step back on Thursday, the index is up about 21% over the past 12 months, trailing only a chip- and artificial intelligence-fueled rally in Taiwan's Taiex among major Asian markets.

Much of the gains reflect the outperformance in Singapore's financial sector, with banks rising in an environment of high rates that has boosted net interest income. DBS, Southeast Asia's largest bank by assets, has climbed more than 50% over the past 12 months, while OCBC and UOB logged gains of more than 30% each. All three banks hit new highs this week.

Going forward, stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data justifies more patience in the Fed's rate-cutting path, with market recalibration for high-for-longer rates driving a more favorable backdrop for the local banks' net interest income ahead, IG market strategist Yeap Jun Rong said.

Singapore could also benefit from the return of a Trump administration in the U.S. and heightened uncertainties about new policies in global trade and other areas.

Without more clarity on new U.S. policies, investors could shift equity allocations more defensively this year, favoring safe havens such as Singapore, Morgan Stanley analysts wrote in a note.

"Singapore tends to outperform during bear markets in Asia/EM when historically it has delivered more positive returns than most other markets," they said.

OCBC Investment Research analysts flagged the possibility of more challenges and volatility "if Trump 1.0 is any indication," saying in a research report that the coming year will likely be dominated by the "triple Ts" of Trump, tariffs and trade.

Another factor this year is Singapore's efforts to beef up the local equities markets, with a central bank report on strengthening equities markets due in August and with possible updates and recommendations coming in phases before then.

"The combination of seemingly stronger political will and low market expectations drives our conviction that soon-to-be-announced initiatives will likely have a meaningfully positive market impact, even if their exact details are still to be fleshed out," Morgan Stanley analysts said.

The new measures are likely to focus on improving stock-trading liquidity, through an injection of capital, and "valuation multiples could rise as much as 20% to narrow the gap to global peers," the analysts said.

Still, the biggest focus in 2025 will be on the U.S. economy and the decisions of the Federal Reserve.

"A more inflationary environment paired with resilient U.S. economic data could reiterate a gradual pace of Fed rate cuts, positioning banks as a top choice for investors seeking strong earnings growth," said IG's Yeap.

Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com

data.title: NZDUSD Technical Analysis – The greenback remains in the driving seat
data.description:
Fundamental Overview

The USD got a boost recently from another set of hot data as the US Job Openings surprised to the upside and the prices paid index in the ISM Services PMI jumped to the highest level since 2023.

The market’s pricing didn’t change much though and Fed’s Waller yesterday kept the rate cuts hopes alive saying that the pace will be driven by inflation progress. Since the central bank has switched its focus back to inflation, the US CPI report next week should have a bigger influence on interest rates expectations than the US NFP tomorrow (barring big deviations).

On the NZD side, the RBNZ cut interest rates by 50 bps as expected at the last meeting. We haven’t got any fresh New Zealand data in the meantime, but the market continues to price in a 76% chance of a 50 bps cut in February and a total of 124 bps of easing by year end.

NZDUSD Technical Analysis – Daily Timeframe

NZDUSD Daily

On the daily chart, we can see that NZDUSD is consolidating near the recent lows with the 2022 low around the 0.5511 level now being very close. We have a downward trendline defining the bearish momentum. The sellers will likely continue to lean on it to position for further downside, while the buyers will look for a break higher to start targeting new highs.

NZDUSD Technical Analysis – 4 hour Timeframe

NZDUSD 4 hour

On the 4 hour chart, there’s not much else we can add here as the price action remains mostly rangebound as we continue to wait for the US NFP and CPI reports. The resistance zone around the 0.5670 level will be a key level to look at if we get there in the next days.

NZDUSD Technical Analysis – 1 hour Timeframe

NZDUSD 1 hour

On the 1 hour chart, we can see that the price is trading at the support zone around the 0.5590 level. The buyers will likely step in here to position for the rally into the resistance zone, while the sellers will look for the price to stay below the support to keep pushing towards the 2022 lows. The red lines define the average daily range for today.

Upcoming Catalysts

Tomorrow, we conclude the week with the US NFP report.

data.title: Copper driven higher by technical factors
data.description:
Copper prices extended gains for a sixth session on Thursday as technical factors provided momentum and outweighed concern over U.S. President-elect Donald Trump's tariff plans for top metals consumer China.

Three-month copper HG1! on the London Metal Exchange (LME) rose 0.7% to $9,096 a metric ton by 1049 GMT after hitting its highest since Dec. 16 at $9,099.

The metal used in power and construction continues to recover from a five-month low of $8,757 touched on Dec. 31. This week brought support on the technical front as it broke above resistance from the 21-day moving average, which now supports at a major psychological level around $9,000.

The market, however, remains worried about how Trump will handle tariff policy after his return to the White House on Jan 20. During the election campaign Trump vowed to impose tariffs of 60% on Chinese imports, but there have since been conflicting reports on the level of potential tariffs.

CNN on Wednesday reported that Trump is considering declaring a national economic emergency to provide legal justification for a series of universal tariffs on allies and adversaries. On Monday the Washington Post said Trump was looking at more nuanced tariffs, which he later denied.

The Chinese yuan, meanwhile, has been hovering around a 16-month low, prompting some Chinese traders to buy copper to try to shield themselves from this weakness and uncertainty about the future, said Ole Hansen, head of commodity strategy at Saxo Bank.

The Yangshan copper premium (SMM-CUYP-CN), which reflects demand for copper imported into China, reached its highest in more than a year at $73 a ton, against $43 two months ago.

In other metals, LME aluminium (CMAl3) rose 1.5% to $2,537 a ton, zinc ZNC1! added 1.4% to $2,864.50, lead LEAD1! gained 0.2% to $1,942, tin FTIN1! edged up 0.2% to $30,110 and nickel NICKEL1! was steady at $15,440.

data.title: UAE Stocks Mixed as Fed Signals Cautious Rate Cuts
data.description:
Emirati stocks closed Thursday's trading session mixed amid concerns over US economic policies under President-elect Donald Trump, including proposed tariffs and immigration measures.

At closing, the FTSE ADX General Index fell 0.033% and the DFM General Index added 0.384%.

Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's December 2024 meeting released Wednesday revealed an overall cautious stance on rate cuts, with inflation concerns tied to Trump's incoming policies. Still, Fed Governor Christopher Waller expressed optimism, predicting inflation will ease in the coming months to support the case for another 25-basis-point rate cut in March 2025.

"Altogether, Governor Waller said that this view of inflation and his expectation that the labor market has stabilized supports further reductions in the Fed's policy rate in 2025, though he said the pace, timing, and magnitude of cuts will ultimately be data-dependent," Morgan Stanley said in a note.

In corporate news, NMDC Energy (ADX:NMDCENR) bagged a $1.14 billion subsea gas pipeline installation and onshore engineering and construction contract for the second phase of Taiwan Power Co.'s Tung-Hsiao power plant renewal project. The energy industry-focused engineering, procurement, and construction company ended trading 2.45% higher.

In Dubai, Al Salam Bank (DFM:SALAM_BAH) fell 0.513% after it announced a partnership with Y K Almoayyed & Sons' electronics and home appliances division to offer exclusive benefits to customers. The offer will run until Oct. 26.

data.title: Clear Channel Outdoor to Sell Europe-North Segment to Bauer Media for $625 Million
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Clear Channel Outdoor Holdings has struck a deal to sell its Europe-North segment to a unit of German media conglomerate Bauer Media Group for $625 million in cash.

Clear Channel on Thursday said the purchase price represents a multiple of around 6.5 times the segment's results for the 12 months ended Sept. 30, adding that it will use part of the proceeds to repay $375 million in term loans.

The deal is slated to close later this year.

Clear Channel, a San Antonio, Texas, provider of advertising billboards and displays, said the sale wraps up the divestiture of the bulk of its European operations as it focuses on the growth of its America and airports segments.

The company previously sold its businesses in Switzerland, Italy and France, but a planned sale of its business in Spain to JCDecaux fell apart late last year over regulatory concerns.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: WeRide to Launch Autonomous Shuttle Service at Zurich Airport
data.description:
WeRide WRD said Thursday it has agreed to deploy its nine-passenger Robobus autonomous shuttle vehicle at Zurich Airport in partnership with Flughafen Zurich AG and Swiss Transit Lab.

The company said the Robobus service, to be launched in Q1, is the first commercial autonomous bus shuttle project at a European airport and will serve employees.

The company said it used its autonomous Robotaxi to map out routes for the shuttle bus at Zurich Airport.

data.title: Tesco Backs Profit Target After Christmas Sales Growth — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Michael Susin

Tesco reiterated its profit target for its fiscal year after reporting sales growth during the Christmas period.

The U.K. supermarket chain on Thursday said it continues to expect retail adjusted operating profit--its preferred metric, which strips out exceptional and other one-off items--in the fiscal year ending in February of around 2.9 billion pounds ($3.59 billion). However, the guidance comes below the market expectations of 3.07 billion pounds, according to a company poll of 13 analysts.

Tesco said like-for-like retail sales rose 3.1% on year in the 19 weeks ended Jan. 4. They rose 2.8% in the third quarter and 3.8% over the Christmas period.

Comparable sales in the U.K., where Tesco is the largest grocer with nearly 30% of the market share according to research firm Kantar, rose 3.9% in the 19-week period and 4.1% during the Christmas period. The increase was driven by volume growth across the period, with a robust contribution from fresh food.

Sales of its premium-range offerings--which included more than 300 festive products--increased 15.5% as British shoppers didn't only buy more but were also willing to pay more than usual to treat themselves better. The latest report from Kantar said nine in 10 households bought at least one premium-range product from a supermarket in December.

During an investors call, Chief Executive Ken Murphy and Chief Financial Imran Nawaz said the sales volume was the key driver of growth and Tesco absorbed part of the inflation costs rather than increasing prices on the counter as part of its efforts to face subdued consumer sentiment. The use of volume scale to boost its market position and reduce costs will be continued, they added.

The executives added that the company will move toward a greater personalization through its loyalty card following a positive response during the festivities with the addition of challenges as part of a gamification strategy. The concept is becoming increasingly present in the sector, with grocers encouraging consumer engagement by offering rewards through elements of game playing such as point scores.

They also said that its retail media--a segment of personalized advertising inside the stores that recently became a new stream of revenue--has been very successful and it continues to be in strong growth. Tesco doesn't disclose the individual figures of this segment, but said it is a contributor to profits and it continues to grow.

Tesco continues to expect retail free cash flow for fiscal 2025 within its medium-term guidance range of 1.4 billion to 1.8 billion pounds. It guides for adjusted operating profit contribution from the retained Tesco Bank business of around 120 million pounds.

The stock fell 1.3% to 36.53 pounds in European morning trading alongside peers such as Sainsbury's and Marks & Spencer.

The fall could relate to a tinge of disappointment that guidance wasn't expanded following the festive success, head of markets at Interactive Investor, Richard Hunter, said in a note.

However, AJ Bell's investment director Russ Mould said the lack of guidance upgrade despite sales growth could reflect Tesco's commitment to keeping prices low and having extra staff during Christmas season in order to increase sales by helping shoppers and build brand loyalty. The grocer could be focusing on a strategy of foregoing a short-term boost to benefit from an improved competitive position in the long term, he said.

In the same day, peer Marks & Spencer reported quarterly sales up 8.7%, mainly driven robust by volume growth in food business. The group launched 500 new lines and sales of new Christmas products grew 14%.

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Linde Announces Fourth Quarter 2024 Earnings and Conference Call Schedule
data.description:
WOKING, UK / ACCESSWIRE / January 9, 2025 / Linde LIN will release its fourth quarter 2024 financial results by 06:00 EST/midday CET on Thursday, February 6, 2025. The Company will host and webcast its conference call at 09:00 EST/15:00 CET, which will be available to the public and the media in listen-only mode.

Live conference call

US Toll-Free Dial-In Number: 1 888 770 7292

UK Toll-Free Dial-In Number: 0800 358 0970

Access code: 6877110

Live webcast (listen-only)

https://www.linde.com/investors/financial-reports

Web replay

Available on demand beginning at 10:30 EST/16:30 CET on

Thursday, February 6, 2025, at:

https://www.linde.com/investors/financial-reports

The earnings release and presentation materials can be accessed on Thursday, February 6, 2025, at https://www.linde.com/investors/financial-reports.

About Linde

Linde is a leading global industrial gases and engineering company with 2023 sales of $33 billion. We live our mission of making our world more productive every day by providing high-quality solutions, technologies and services which are making our customers more successful and helping to sustain, decarbonize and protect our planet.

The company serves a variety of end markets such as chemicals & energy, food & beverage, electronics, healthcare, manufacturing, metals and mining. Linde's industrial gases and technologies are used in countless applications including production of clean hydrogen and carbon capture systems critical to the energy transition, life-saving medical oxygen and high-purity & specialty gases for electronics. Linde also delivers state-of-the-art gas processing solutions to support customer expansion, efficiency improvements and emissions reductions.

For more information about the company and its products and services, please visit www.linde.com

data.title: JonDeTech Sensors, Millenium Semiconductors Enter Distribution Deal in India
data.description:
JonDeTech Sensors (JDT.ST) signed a distribution agreement with India-based electronic components distributor Millenium Semiconductors, according to a Thursday release.

The deal allows the distribution of the Swedish sensor technology supplier's entire product portfolio in India. It is initially set for three years, with an option for renewal.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

Thousands of firefighters and emergency personnel in Los Angeles spent a second night battling flames and evacuating residents as a series of rampant wildfires raged almost unchecked, raising the daunting question of just how long it will take the city to control the blaze. The disaster complicates what was already a make-or-break year for California's dysfunctional home-insurance market.

Today's Headlines

In America's most populated county, at least five separate fires burned and fresh evacuation orders were issued overnight, upending the lives of tens of thousands, and destroying property.

Washington will pause preparations for a new president to honor Jimmy Carter, whose legacy as the 39th president is entangled in many ways with the present-day ambitions of the 47th.

Donald Trump's latest demands of America's NATO partners — that they cede territory to the U.S. and spend more on defense than Washington itself does — risk undermining allies' confidence and potentially emboldening adversaries.

Israel's defense industry is booming, spurred by foreign demand for its aerial-defense systems and the country's ongoing military campaigns.

U.S.-based Moderna is planting roots in China, a sales opportunity that comes with complicated dynamics inside China and tense politics back home.

Live From The Markets

Wildfires are making investors nervous about the Los Angeles area's utility company again, writes Jonathan Weil.

Blackstone is investing $300 million into artificial-intelligence data company DDN.

U.S. companies and investors in Japan are at risk of becoming less welcome after President Biden blocked the U.S. Steel deal, writes Jacky Wong.

Read It Here First

California was in a home-insurance crisis before the Los Angeles inferno.

Even before the current fires were sparked, millions of homeowners in the Golden State faced double-digit insurance rate hikes, non-renewals or a dearth of any available private coverage. Leading insurers had stopped selling new home-insurance policies, saying rate increases approved by regulators were insufficient to cover their losses, including from the devastating wildfires of 2017 and 2018. The economic toll of the fires could prove a serious blow to a market already in trouble.

American Airlines is trying to win back American corporations.

American, the nation's second-largest airline, missed out on $1.5 billion in revenue last year in a failed bid to overhaul how it competes for business travel. After a monthslong mea culpa from its executives, the airline is restoring some perks, introducing deep discounts and rebuilding its sales ranks to win over what had been one of its most reliable and lucrative groups of customers, Alison Sider reports.

Expert Take

Q: What's driving the intense L.A. wildfires?

Warm, dry winds known as the Santa Anas are behind the devastating fires across the Los Angeles region. Eric Niiler explains how these gusts occur and why they've become especially problematic this year.

A: Santa Ana winds occur in winter months when a high-pressure system develops in the Great Basin of the interior West at the same time that a low-pressure system sets up just off the Pacific. The difference in pressure creates the winds.

Santa Anas move in the opposite direction of the normal onshore flow that carries cool, moist air from the Pacific Ocean into the region. These winds blow from the deserts of interior California and Nevada out to the ocean. As the winds are forced through the narrow canyons and valleys of the Pacific Coast mountains, they heat up, lose moisture and pick up speed.

Santa Anas are feared by Californians because they are fast-moving, dry and extremely warm-conditions that can turn a tiny spark into a raging blaze that is difficult for firefighters to control. This year, these winds have arrived on the heels of two extremely rainy years that spurred the growth of brush and grasses. Southern California then experienced a particularly hot summer in 2024, followed by almost no rain during the wet season, which began in October. Those dried-out plants are now serving as fuel for wildfires.

See The Story

These border cities thrived under Trump's first term. His new policies threaten to undo them.

Detroit and Laredo, Texas, two cities perched on the borders of the U.S.'s biggest trading partners, are on the front lines of the nation's post-Covid trade economy. An uptick in nearshoring, alongside other factors, helped transform Laredo into a heavyweight in global trade. Detroit, meanwhile, is expanding its already brisk automotive trade with Canada. That economic order is likely to be disrupted by Trump's vow to place 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico on the first day he takes office.

Happening Today

The funeral of former President Jimmy Carter takes place in Washington, D.C. The U.S. stock market is closed and bond markets will close early at 2 p.m. ET.

Earnings: Aritzia

Number Of The Day:

99

Israel's official count of hostages in Gaza, after the remains of Yosef AlZayadni, 53, were found alongside the bodies of his captors. AlZayadni, a Bedouin Arab citizen of Israel, was kidnapped by Hamas-led militants during the Oct. 7, 2023, attack.

And Finally...

There's a glimmer of hope in 2025 for frustrated jobseekers.

More business leaders expect the global economy to improve in the first half of this year — and optimistic executives are more likely to hire. Recruiters and executives also say some companies slowed or paused hiring last year to assess the capabilities and limitations of AI — and concluded they'll need more human workers in the coming year, not fewer.

data.title: UK consumer confidence falters after Christmas splurge
data.description:
Key points:
  • Prospect of higher taxes and inflation sow consumer caution
  • M&S said had best-performing Christmas sales
  • Tesco says consumers always retrench in January
  • Says pandemic has delivered lessons in adapting to disruption

Shares in Britain's Marks & Spencer and other retailers fell on Thursday, with 2 billion pounds ($2.45 billion) wiped off the sector, as concern about ebbing consumer confidence and economic weakness overshadowed healthy Christmas trading.

Retailers, already facing weak consumer sentiment, are bracing for higher costs from April, when employer taxes and the minimum wage are set to rise.

The economic outlook has been clouded by a leap in Britain's government borrowing costs in recent days that adds to pressure on government finances and has prompted analyst warnings that further tax rises could be needed.

With inflation also forecast to tick up, retailers anticipate a tough year.

"There is that cautious customer confidence out there," M&S Chief Executive Stuart Machin told reporters, after announcing the group had delivered the highest food sales over the lucrative Christmas period on the UK high street.

M&S reported above-expectations growth of 8.9% in food sales and 1.9% in clothing, home and beauty sales, but the retailer's shares fell 6.5%. Tesco TSCO, the country's biggest supermarket group, posted a 4.1% rise in sales, while its shares traded down 1.3%.

"The year ahead won't be all smooth sailing for the retail giants, as the sector gears up to battle imminent tax hikes," Hargreaves Lansdown equity analyst Matt Britzman said.

While those two retailers were helped by booming grocery sales, other categories struggled.

Growth at food-on-the-go specialist Greggs slowed in the final months of 2024 and discounter B&M posted a fall in underlying sales of 2.8%, sending the stocks down by 10% and 12% respectively.

While retailers fell, Britain's globally focused blue-chip index CURRENCYCOM:UK100 traded higher at 0.5%.

CHALLENGES CONTINUE

Greggs Chief Executive Roisin Currie said consumers were cautious about spending.

"It's been a challenging second half in 2024. I think you have to make some assumptions that that continues in 2025," she told Reuters.

Greggs had performed well in recent years as its value sausage rolls and steak bakes gained popularity, but its underlying sales growth fell to 2.5% in the final quarter of 2024, down from 5% in the previous period.

Next, the UK's biggest clothing retailer by market capitalisation, on Tuesday warned sales growth would slow in its 2025/26 year as the impact of the government's tax hike begins to hit employment levels and raise prices.

Ken Murphy, the boss of Tesco, was more sanguine.

Although consumers who "really celebrated over Christmas" would be more value-focused in January, that was always the case at the beginning of the year, he said.

After the pandemic, a supply chain crisis, and high levels of commodity and energy inflation, Murphy said Tesco, which is forecasting 250 million pounds of additional costs from the employer tax hikes, was used to handling rising costs.

($1 = 0.8132 pounds)

data.title: U.S. Henry Hub Natural Gas Spot Price Hits Historic Low in 2024, EIA Reports
data.description:
The average U.S. benchmark Henry Hub natural gas spot price hit a historic low of US$2.21 per million British thermal units in 2024, the U.S. Energy Information Administration reported Wednesday.

This was the lowest average annual price in inflation-adjusted dollars ever reported, the EIA noted. The annual average Henry Hub price in 2024 decreased by 16% from its 2023 average and 68% from its 2022 average, the largest two-year decline on record.

The monthly average Henry Hub price ranged from US$3.25/MMBtu in January to an all-time low of US$1.51/MMBtu in March.

On an inflation-adjusted basis, average monthly prices in February, March, April and August were the four lowest ever recorded, and the four lowest daily prices ever recorded also occurred during 2024, the EIA said.

The price drop can be attributed to robust U.S. natural gas supply and demand constraints, the agency said. Relatively strong U.S. gas production and more moderate weather in February and March also reduced the gas price steadily.

Relatively full storage also contributed to lower natural gas prices in the first quarter of 2024. At the end of the season of gas withdrawals from storage on March 31, inventories totaled 2,306 billion cubic feet, 25% more than the year-ago period and 39% more than the five-year average, according to the EIA.

Flat U.S. natural gas net exports and little change in U.S. liquefied natural gas capacity limited demand growth, on top of lower gas consumption in the residential and commercial sectors as the need for seasonal space heating lessened.

The U.S. natural gas market entered the winter of 2024-2025 with the most natural gas in storage since 2016, the agency noted.

data.title: PolarCool Wins Concussion Treatment Deal with Czech Ice Hockey Club
data.description:
PolarCool (POLAR.ST) signed a contract with Czech ice hockey team BK Mladá Boleslav for its concussion treatment PolarCap.

Under the agreement, the Swedish medical device company's PolarCap system will be integrated into the team's recovery protocols, according to a Thursday release.

The deal marks PolarCool's first contract in the Czech Republic's top ice hockey league, Extraliga.

data.title: Delixi New Energy Technology Plans to Apply for Up to 300-Million-Yuan Loan
data.description:
Delixi New Energy Technology 603032 plans to apply for a loan of up to 300 million yuan from Industrial Bank to meet its development plan and funding needs.

The lithium battery equipment manufacturer said it will use self-owned assets as collateral for a loan of up to 91 million yuan, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse. The loan has a term of up to five years.

Shares of Delixi New Energy Technology closed 2% higher Thursday.

data.title: Hong Kong sees no need to change US dollar-pegged currency system
data.description:
Key points:
  • Hong Kong dollar pegged in tight band versus US unit
  • Recent rally has brought it close to testing upper end of band
  • Hong Kong's monetary authority chief says can defend the system

Hong Kong has no intention and sees no need to change the system that pegs the city's currency in a tight band to the U.S. dollar and has the ability to defend it, the chief executive of Hong Kong's de facto central bank said on Thursday.

Eddie Yue made the remarks amid recent strength in the Hong Kong dollar USDHKD, which surged to a 3-1/2 year high against the U.S. currency last week, not far from testing the strong end of the system's trading band.

Under Hong Kong's Linked Exchange Rate System (LERS), the financial hub's currency is confined to a range between 7.75 and 7.85 to the greenback, and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) is committed to intervening to maintain the band.

"Despite the recent interest in LERS and even speculation regarding potential geopolitical shocks, the Hong Kong dollar market has continued to operate smoothly in accordance with the design of the LERS," Yue said in a statement posted on HKMA's website.

"And let me reiterate, we have no intention and we see no need to change the LERS."

The financial hub has sizeable foreign reserves of over $420 billion, equivalent to about 1.7 times its monetary base, which Yue said meant "ensuring the smooth functioning of the LERS at all times".

A string of factors, including seasonal funding shortages, buying by mainland Chinese investors and listed companies' increasing dividend payments contributed to the tight liquidity in Hong Kong and underpinned the currency, traders and analysts said.

Yue said the HKMA was paying close attention to discussions about the exchange rate system, which has weathered numerous economic cycles and multiple financial crises.

"As a small, open economy and major international financial centre, exchange rate stability is crucial for Hong Kong," Yue said, dismissing the view that a strengthening Hong Kong dollar alongside the greenback would hinder the city's economic recovery.

Analysts at Barclays expect the Hong Kong dollar to stay close to 7.75 per dollar in January, but look for it to weaken subsequently.

"We think global factors are likely to keep sentiment subdued and support USD/HKD, especially after the positive impulse from dividend payouts by HK-listed firms and (as) IPO activity fades," they said in a note published this week.

"The onshore buying of Hong Kong stocks may continue due to lack of better investment alternatives, but it would need more foreign participants to buy Hong Kong stocks for HKD demand to be lifted more durably."

data.title: UK firms to raise prices and cut jobs after tax hike, BoE survey shows
data.description:
Key points:
  • Survey shows firms plan price hikes, job cuts due to tax rise
  • UK business sentiment, hiring and investment plans have declined since budget
  • BoE monitoring inflation impact, cautious on interest rate cuts

British businesses expect to raise prices and reduce staff numbers in response to an increase in employers' social security contributions that will take effect in April, a Bank of England survey of more than 2,000 firms showed on Thursday.

The BoE's Decision Maker Panel showed 61% of companies expected reduced profits, 54% planned to raise prices, 53% expected lower employment and 39% intended smaller pay rises due to the rise in National Insurance set out in Oct. 30's budget.

Others surveys have also shown a slide in business sentiment and hiring and investment intentions since finance minister Rachel Reeves announced a 25 billion-pound ($31 billion) increase in payroll taxes.

The slowdown in the economy has contributed to worries in financial markets about Britain's public debt levels which has pushed up borrowing costs sharply this week .

Separate figures on Thursday from an association of recruitment agencies showed demand for new staff had fallen by the most since August 2020.

The BoE - which is considering when to cut interest rates again - is watching for whether the higher employment costs feed into inflation through price rises, or job cuts, reduced investment and wage growth which would slow the economy.

Rob Wood, chief UK economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics, said the BoE's survey suggested the tax rises were feeding more into prices - and less into a slowdown - than the REC or S&P purchasing managers' index surveys were reporting.

"The core DMP (survey) questions continue to signal stubborn inflation and wage growth, and a less severe weakening in the jobs market than qualitative surveys show, which should keep the Monetary Policy Committee cutting interest rates only 'gradually'," he said.

British consumer price inflation rose to an eight-month high of 2.6% in November and the BoE expects it to rise further in 2025 and not return to its 2% target until 2027 - limiting its willingness to cut interest rates from their current 4.75%.

The BoE survey, conducted between Dec. 6 and Dec. 20, showed firms planned to raise prices by 3.8% over the next 12 months, 0.1 percentage points more than expected in the three months to November.

Expected year-ahead wage growth remained unchanged at 4.0% on a three-month moving-average basis in December.

($1 = 0.8141 pounds)

data.title: UK rout: born in the USA?
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 up 0.2%
  • Retailers weak
  • Miners, energy higher
  • Wall St futures lower

UK ROUT: BORN IN THE USA?

As UK government borrowing costs spike and sterling tumbles against rival currencies, the Labour leadership is coming under extra pressure to cut spending or raise taxes, in a gloomy signal for the economy.

But some analysts think this latest UK rout could be short lived, as it has been driven by higher U.S. yields and a surging dollar ahead of President-elect Donald Trump taking office.

Nick Rees, senior FX market analyst at Monex, said:

"These latest sterling moves (are) an overreaction to gilts hitting some key psychological levels last seen when Liz Truss was in office."

"Granted this has taken place against a backdrop where the pound is under pressure for a stronger dollar and yields are rising. But this is a function of markets preparing for Trump to take office, not a verdict on the government’s credibility, at least for now."

But traders are also bracing for big swings in the pound, as shown by sterling-dollar one-month implied volatility hitting its highest since March 2023 on Thursday, which Rees said was probably going to become a widespread trend across foreign exchange markets.

"The incoming Trump administration is going to shake things up and that does warrant higher FX volatility.

"We think implied volatility across other currencies now needs to play catch up with the rise seen for sterling in recent days."

(Naomi Rovnick)

data.title: Room for U.S. equities and credit to outperform on tariff news - JPM
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 flat
  • Retailers weak
  • Miners, energy higher
  • Wall St futures lower

ROOM FOR U.S. EQUITIES AND CREDIT TO OUTPERFORM ON TARIFF NEWS - JPM

Market gyrations in recent days have showcased how sensitive prices are to tariff-related headlines. For JP Morgan quantitative strategists, it looks like there is still room for U.S. equities, credit and even industrials stocks to outperform on tariff news.

In a note, they say over the past week tariffs-related press reports have prompted questions in their conversations over how such risks have been priced in markets.

To attempt to answer, they compare market moves during Trump's 2018 tariff episode to today's, using Aug 1 2024 as a starting point for the latest episode.

First: the dollar. JPM says the positioning build up in the current juncture has been between a quarter and a third of the 2018 moves.

"Between Apr 3rd and Dec 1st 2018 the dollar broad effective exchange rate appreciated by nearly 9%. By comparison, the market moves since Aug 1st, the broad dollar has now appreciated by just under 3.5%, or around 40% of the 2018 period."

Elsewhere, U.S. equities have been outperforming since August, they say, by around 55% versus non-US equities.

Energy, materials and industrials were among the worst performing sectors during the 2018 episode and have also underperformed since August 1st. But notably, energy and materials' underperformance has been deeper this time around while industrials' adjustment has been more modest.

"In bond markets, for 10y UST yields vs. Bunds the relative rise in the spread of around 47bp since Aug 1st 2024 is somewhat larger than the rise of 40bp from Apr 3rd to Dec 1st 2018," write the quants.

JPM thinks this would suggest that there is more room for U.S. equities, credit and industrials to outperform other markets on tariff news.

(Lucy Raitano)

data.title: European stocks stabilise as miners offset retail gloom
data.description:
Key points:
  • B&M down after retailer narrows profit forecast
  • Tecan Group up after annual sales beat expectations
  • Tesco slips on retaining profit outlook
  • STOXX 600 inches up

European stocks shed early losses to edge higher on Thursday, as gains in miners offset declines in retailers although uncertainty about the monetary policy path and U.S. tariff plans kept bond markets under pressure.

The pan-European STOXX 600 SXXP inched up 0.1% as of 1036 GMT, reversing early declines of about 0.5%.

Concern about rising inflation, reduced chances of a drop in interest rates, uncertainty over how U.S. president-elect Donald Trump will conduct foreign or economic policy, and the prospect of trillions of dollars in extra debt sales have sent bond yields soaring around the world this week.

A report about U.S. President-elect Donald Trump considering declaring a national economic emergency to provide legal justification for a series of universal tariffs also hurt market sentiment.

Investors were squarely focussed on Trump's Jan. 20 inauguration to check whether he sticks to his rhetoric on protectionist measures and their implications on the European economy.

"There is hope right now that Trump's threats are more of a tool to initiate negotiations with a punch rather than a complete endgame where he would definitely impose tariffs on Europeans," said Ipek Ozkardeskaya, senior market analyst at Swissquote Bank.

"So there is this hope that the things could not get as bad as he pretends they could."

Helping the mood somewhat on Thursday, the German 10-year yield (DE10YT=RR) retreated from its session high to trade at 2.53% but still held near its multi-month peak.

Britain continued to remain at the heart of the bond selloff, with the benchmark 10-year yield (GB10YT=RR) touching a fresh 2008 high.

The UK mid-cap index (.FTMC) tumbled 0.6% to an eight-month low.

Retail stocks (.SXRP) were at the forefront of losses, dragged by B&M's BME 13% fall after the British discount retailer lowered the top end of its annual profit forecast on Thursday.

Other retail stocks in the UK such as Marks & Spencer MKS, Tesco TSCO and Greggs GRG fell 6.4%, 1.3% and 10.2%, respectively, following disappointing Christmas trading update.

Countering losses on the index was the mining sector (.SXPP) that soared 1.8%, with heavyweight miners Rio Tinto RIO, Anglo American AAL and Antofagasta ANTO leading gains.

A 0.8% rise in the oil and gas sector (.SXEP) also supported the index.

Tecan Group TECN rose 6.8% after the Swiss life science equipment maker posted annual 2024 sales that surpassed market expectations.

Trading volumes in Europe were lighter than usual, with U.S. markets closed on Thursday to mark a national day of mourning following the death of former President Jimmy Carter.

data.title: Spanish Industrial Pessimism Eases
data.description:
The seasonally adjusted industry confidence indicator in Spain rose by 0.5 points from the previous month to -4.6 in December 2024.

This improvement was driven by a decline in stocks of finished products (7.3 vs 10.5) and a smaller fall in new orders (-6.5 vs -9.7).

However, production expectations declined (-0.1 vs 4.8).
